exemplar.title: Investigating The Influence Of The Primacy Effect On Personality Impression Formation

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating The Influence Of The Primacy Effect On Personality Impression Formation

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clearly stated aim and relevance with strong context for a high-school setting
Thorough description of the primacy-effect model with explicit links to the IV and planned replication
Well-operationalized hypotheses with both IV and DV clearly defined and directional as well as null hypotheses presented
weaknesses: Control condition in the null hypothesis is not fully described
Incomplete citation details for one theoretical reference (Solomon E.)
Minor structural suggestions (e.g. previewing hypotheses) remain unaddressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of the introduction meet the 5â6 band descriptors: a clear aim with explained relevance, a well-described theory/model with explicit connection, and fully operationalized hypotheses.  Minor citation and description gaps prevent a perfect 6/6, warranting a solid 5/6.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but well justified (avoiding order effects, aligning with the original study)
Convenience sampling is clearly described and its implications for validity and generalisability are explained
Procedure details (consent, demographics, adjective sets, debriefing) are thorough
weaknesses: Controlled variables are named but lack systematic explanation of how each was managed
Ethical considerations omit mention of formal ethics board approval
Gender imbalance is noted without supporting literature or data to argue negligible impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design and sampling both achieve the top band (4/4) through clear explanation, while materials and control of variables sit at the lower end of the 3â4 band but still meet its descriptor.  Therefore a full 4/4 is awarded.

strengths: Appropriate use of both descriptive (percentages) and inferential (binomial test) statistics
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with the correct chart type
Findings are linked to the hypothesis in the text
weaknesses: Misinterpretation of pâ=â0.054 as significant and omission of control-group data in analysis
Graph lacks title, axis labels, scale markers and proper legend formatting
Limited depth in numerical discussion (no effect sizes or variability measures)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall into the 3â4 bandâappropriate methods used but contain errors in application or presentation and limited interpretationâso the criterion earns a 3/6.

strengths: Findings are explicitly related to Asch (1946) and Anderson (1961), embedding results in theory
Strengths and limitations are detailed, relevant and linked to validity/generalisation
Modifications are fully justified, each tied directly to identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion of findings remains brief and largely confirmatory without deeper critical engagement
Alternative phenomena (e.g. anchoring bias) are mentioned but not fully integrated
Online administrationâs potential distractions/fatigue not explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (strengths/limitations and modifications) meet the highest band, while findings discussion is solid but lacks depth, placing it in the middle band.  The weighted average merits a 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart directly contrasts yes/no responses across conditions, so it addresses the hypothesis. However, the chart lacks an informative title, labelled axes, and correct legend formatting â clear presentational errors that place it in the 3-4 band.

The student links statistical findings to the hypothesis, stating the null was rejected and discussing directionality, hence interpretation is present. Yet the significance decision is incorrect and numerical discussion minimal, so errors warrant the 3-4 band.

Both IV (order: positive-first vs negative-first) and DV (yes/no willingness to work) are stated and operationalised in concrete terms. Separate null and directional hypotheses are presented, each referencing the operational IV and DV, so the highest band is appropriate.

Convenience sampling is detailed (115 high-school students, age range, gender imbalance) and the implications for generalisability and ecological validity are explained. The choice of participants is therefore justified, satisfying the explanation level.

The primacy-effect model is outlined with citations to Asch (1946) and Anderson (1961/65); the mechanism is described and its everyday relevance illustrated. The student explicitly links this theory to the planned replication and to the chosen IV (order of adjectives). This meets the descriptor of a full description plus explicit connection.

Findings are compared with Asch (1946) and Anderson (1961), demonstrating an explicit link to background theory. The discussion remains brief and largely confirmatory, without deeper critical engagement, suiting the middle band.

The student states a clear aim â to investigate the influence of the primacy effect on personality-impression formation â and explicitly explains why this is relevant in a high-school context (polarised judgements, same age as participants, increased ecological validity). Both components (âwhatâ and âwhy it mattersâ) are present and sufficiently elaborated, matching the 5-6 band.

Several relevant strengths (large sample size) and limitations (convenience sampling, gender imbalance, online setting, dichotomous DV) are explained and tied to validity/generalisation. The level of detail and direct relevance meet the highest band.

Materials (online questionnaire, adjective list pre-rated, yes/no scale) and several potential confounds are discussed, showing some rationale. However, controlled variables are only generally referenced, and no systematic explanation of how each was managed is provided. This aligns with the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Proposed changes (use rating scales for sensitivity, broaden age range, investigate related biases) are explicitly linked to the identified limitations and are justified in terms of how they would improve validity and scope, satisfying the criteria for full justification.

The student explains why an independent-measures design was chosen (avoid order effects, align with original study, control-group complications). This goes beyond mere description and therefore fits the top band for Exploration.

Both descriptive (percentages) and inferential (binomial test) statistics are used appropriately for dichotomous data. Nonetheless, the student misinterprets p = 0.054 as significant, and the control group data are not analysed. Such errors keep it within the âappropriate but containing errorsâ band.

annotations.comment: The introduction header is prominently placed and clearly signals the start of the investigation.
The null hypothesis refers to a control group that is not clearly described; define the control condition explicitly.
Rephrase the directional hypothesis to specify expected magnitude or direction (e.g. âparticipants in the positive-first group will be X% more likely to choose âyesââ).
Sampling technique is clearly described (convenience sampling, age range, gender breakdown).
Gender imbalance is acknowledged but no literature is cited to justify its negligible impact; provide citations or data.
Choice of an independent-measures design is well justified by original study alignment and avoidance of order effects.
Procedure description is thorough, detailing consent, demographics, adjective sets, and debriefing.
Rationale for dichotomous yes/no measure is given but needs acknowledgement of reduced sensitivity and potential response bias.
Ethical considerations note anonymity and consent but omit mention of institutional review or ethics board approval.
Theoretical introduction cites âSolomon E.â without full reference details; include year and complete citation to strengthen academic rigor.
Consider previewing the studyâs hypotheses in the introduction to guide the readerâs expectations and structure.
The description of the primacy effect is thorough, with clear citations to foundational studies (Asch, 1946; Anderson, 1965).
Linking the primacy effect to everyday examples (elections, decision making) strengthens the studyâs relevance.
In the âVariablesâ section, the dependent variable is missing. Include both IV and DV definitions together for clarity.
Dependent variable (âyes/noâ response) is precisely operationalized, enabling clear measurement of impression formation.
Descriptive analysis relies solely on group percentages; include variability measures or effect sizes for depth.
The table is captioned as âFigure 1â but is formatted as a table â use accurate labels to avoid confusion.
Add a descriptive title and clearly label rows/columns so the table can be interpreted without the main text.
Bar chart lacks axis labels, scale markers, and legend; this obscures the comparison between conditions.
The reported p = 0.0537 exceeds the Î± â¤ 0.05 threshold; the null hypothesis should not have been rejected.
Define âsuccessâ criteria for the binomial test in the main text rather than referring only to the appendix.
Strengths and limitations are directly tied to validity and generalisability, showing depth of evaluation.
Expand the limitations to discuss how online administration might introduce distractions or fatigue that affect responses.
Findings are well linked to Asch (1946) and Anderson (1961), embedding results within theoretical context.
Alternative phenomena (anchoring bias, serial-position effect) are mentioned but not fully integrated into the discussion; elaborate links.
Propose specific future modificationsâsuch as mixed-methods interviews or varied adjective intensitiesâclearly tied to identified limitations.
